content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 5, 2010


Jason Kaune
Nielsen Merksamer Parrinello
Mueller & Naylor, LLP
2350 Kerner Blvd, Suite 250
San Rafael, CA 94901

Re:    Your Request for Informal Assistance
       Our File No. I-10-052

Dear Mr. Kaune:

     This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the San Joaquin Common
Sense Coalition (a recently formed project of Citizens for Reliable and Affordable Electricity, a
California public benefit nonprofit corporation, and Pacific Gas and Electric Company)
regarding the campaign reporting provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

     Please be aware that the Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct.
(Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate
any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions provided apply only to
prospective actions.

     Moreover, the Commission will only provide formal written advice to a person whose
duties are under the Act or to that person's authorized representative. The
Commission does not provide advice to a third party about a public official's duties under the
Act. (Section 83114; regulation 18329.) Thus, we cannot respond to your specific questions
concerning the duties of the South San Joaquin Irrigation District.

     However, we can provide informal assistance with respect to your client's prospective
obligations under the Act. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity
provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3).)

_______________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. I-10-052
Page 2

QUESTIONS

(1)(a) Should payments for communications to the public that urge local citizens to
oppose the "change of services" application filed with the San Joaquin LAFCO by the South San
Joaquin Irrigation District be disclosed under the Act even if the proposal is not initiated by a
petition?

(1)(b) Should payments for billboards, websites, mailings, radio, and newspaper
advertisements, movie theater advertisements, phone calls, and other communications to the
public that urge local citizens to oppose a "change of services" application filed with the San
Joaquin LAFCO by the South San Joaquin Irrigation District be disclosed?

(2) When do such disclosure obligations begin? Is the disclosure triggered when
expenses have been incurred or when communications are made to oppose the proposal?

(3) Should the disclosures be filed monthly, semi annually, or quarterly?

(4) What forms and types of statements should be filed?

(5) Should amounts disclosed include the payments to public relations firms and
campaign consultants, and compensation to employees when engaging in direct contacts and
grassroots lobbying when made to influence the action of LAFCO?

FACTS

The South San Joaquin Irrigation District (the "district") is a special district and
governmental entity that provides irrigation and domestic water services to South San Joaquin
County. On September 3, 2009, the district's Board of Directors approved a Resolution of
Application to submit an application to San Joaquin LAFCO. Under this application, the district
would expand its function to providing retail electricity to those living within the district's
boundaries.²

You represent the San Joaquin Common Sense Coalition (the "coalition"), a recently
formed project of Citizens for Reliable and Affordable Electricity, a California public benefit
nonprofit corporation, and Pacific Gas and Electric Company. The coalition opposes the
application. However, while the coalition plans on opposing the application by means of
billboard advertisements, website messages, mailings, radio, and newspaper advertisements,
movie theater advertisements, phone banks, and other communications to the public, the

² In your request for advice, you go into significant detail describing the activity of the district. As noted
above, the commission will not advise with respect to past conduct or provide third-party advice. (Regulation
18329.) Obviously, the activity of a public agency in connection with its own application to LAFCO presents
significantly different questions and analysis than such activity by a private corporation or group of citizens. Thus,
we do not address the obligations of the South San Joaquin Irrigation District and nothing in this letter should be
construed to be commentary on the district's responsibility. If you believe there has been a violation of the Act, you
may wish to file a complaint with the Commission's Enforcement Division.

File No. I-10-052
Page 3

coalition has not yet commenced distributing these communications. Your letter seeks
clarification of the coalition's duties once they begin distributing the communications.
ANALYSIS

Qualification: Generally, individuals and organizations will not have campaign
reporting obligations under the Act, unless they qualify as a "committee." Section 82013 states
that a committee is any person or combination of persons who directly or indirectly does any of
the following:

Section 82013 provides:

"Committee" means any person or combination of persons who directly or
indirectly does any of the following:

"(a) Receives contributions totaling one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more
in a calendar year;

"(b) Makes independent expenditures totaling one thousand dollars
($1,000) or more in a calendar year; or

"(c) Makes contributions totaling ten thousand dollars ($10,000) or more
in a calendar year to or at the behest of candidates or committees. A person or
combination of persons that becomes a committee shall retain its status as a
committee until such time as that status is terminated pursuant to Section 84214."

The terms "contribution" and "expenditure" are defined as payments "unless it is clear
from surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political purposes." An "independent
expenditure" means an expenditure made by any person in connection with a communication
which expressly advocates the election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate or the
qualification, passage or defeat of a clearly identified measure, or taken as a whole and in
context, unambiguously urges a particular result in an election but which is not made to or at the
behest of the affected candidate or committee. (Section 82031.)

In 2008, AB 1998 (Stats. 2008, ch. 192, § 1) added Section 84251 to the Act. Section
84251 provides: "A payment made for 'political purposes,' as that term is used in Sections
82015 and 82025, includes a payment made for the purpose of influencing or attempting to
influence the actions of voters or a local agency formation commission or for the
qualification, adoption, or passage of a LAFCO proposal." 3 The stated purpose for the bill was

3 Prior to the 2008 addition of the specific LAFCO provisions to the Act, the rule was as follows: "The
obligation to report expenditures in support of or in opposition to a proposal for incorporation begins when the
proposal becomes a measure pursuant to Section 82043. A proposal for incorporation becomes a measure when the
board of supervisors orders the proposal on the ballot. (In re Fontana (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 25.) At that time,
contributions received to support or oppose the measure were reportable. Further, contributions received and
expenditures made before the proposal became a measure that were for the purpose of influencing citizens to vote
for or against the measure were recaptured in the initial campaign statement filed once the proposal became a

File No. I-10-052
Page 4

to "impose on a committee formed to support or oppose a LAFCO proposal, as defined,
requirements regarding the filing of campaign statements. The bill would require the committee
to file monthly campaign statements from the time circulation of a petition begins until a
measure is placed on the ballot or the committee is terminated. After a LAFCO proposal
measure is placed on the ballot, the bill would require a committee formed to support or oppose
the proposal to file those campaign statements required of other committees formed to support or
oppose ballot measures under the Political Reform Act of 1974.

Thus, if any group of persons received $1,000 in payments for the purpose of influencing
or attempting to influence the votes of voters or a local agency formation commission for or
against the qualification, adoption, or passage of a LAFCO proposal, they would qualify as a
committee under Section 82013(a) and have reporting requirements. Similarly, a person may
also qualify as a committee under 82013(b) by making $1,000 in independent expenditures
(expenditures made in connection with a communication expressly advocating the qualification,
adoption, or passage of a clearly identified LAFCO proposal, or taken as a whole and in context,
unambiguously urges a particular result for a LAFCO proposal) and under 82013(c) by making
$10,000 in contributions in connection with the LAFCO proceeding. Section 84250 specifically
states that "[a]ll requirements of this title applicable to a measure, as defined in Section 82043,
84252."

In 2009, AB 528 (Stats. 2009, ch. 113, § 4) further expanded reporting in connection
with LAFCO proposals by conforming the application of the Act's reporting provisions with the
definitions in the Cortese-Knox-Hertzberg Act (Government Code Sections 56000 et seq.)
governing LAFCOs. Under AB 1998, a LAFCO proposal was defined as: "[a] proposal, as
defined in Section 56069, that is initiated as a petition for filing with a local agency formation
commission." (Emphasis added.) AB 528 redefined "proposal" in Section 82035.5 of the Act as
"a proposal, as defined in Section 56069, including a proceeding, as defined by Section 56067."
These Government Code sections, which are not part of the Act, provide respectively:

" 'Proceeding,' 'proceeding for a change of organization,' or 'proceeding
for a reorganization' means proceedings taken by the commission for a proposed
change of organization or reorganization pursuant to Part 4 (commencing with
Section 57000)." (Section 56067.)

" 'Proposal' means a request or statement of intention made by petition or
by resolution of application of a legislative body or of a school district proposing
proceedings for the change of organization or reorganization described in the
request or statement of intention. (Section 56069, emphasis added.)

measure." (Roberts Advice Letter, No. A-98-125.)

AB 528 also added a reference to Section 56067. However, the reference is to a more specific statute that
is "included" within the broad language of Section 56069 and does not provide further guidance on the scope of
Section 56069.

File No. I-10-052
Page 5

Thus, the obligation to report contributions and expenditures in connection with LAFCO
proceedings apply not merely to petitions filed with LAFCO, but also any resolution of
application filed with LAFCO.

Reporting Contributions and Expenditures: Once the group qualifies as a
"committee" under Section 82013(a), it will be required to file periodic campaign reports.
Section 84252 provides as follows:

"(a) A committee primarily formed to support or oppose a LAFCO
proposal shall file all statements required under this chapter except that, in lieu of
the statements required by Sections 84200 and 84202.3, the committee shall file
monthly campaign statements from the time circulation of a petition begins until a
measure is placed on the ballot or, if a measure is not placed on the ballot, until
the committee is terminated pursuant to Section 84214. The committee shall file
an original and one copy of each statement on the 15th day of each calendar
month, covering the prior calendar month, with the clerk of the county in which
the measure may be voted on. If the petition results in a measure that is placed on
the ballot, the committee thereafter shall file campaign statements required by this
chapter.

"(b) In addition to any other statements required by this chapter, a
committee that makes independent expenditures in connection with a LAFCO
proposal shall file statements pursuant to Section 84203.5."

QUESTIONS AND CONCLUSIONS

(1)(a) Should payments for communications to the public that urge local citizens to
oppose the "change of services" application be disclosed under the Act even if the proposal is
not initiated by a petition?

As you noted in your request letter, AB 528 amended Section 82035.5 to remove the
language limiting a "LAFCO proposal" to those proposals "initiated by a petition for filing with a
local agency information commission." The statute now reads (with the existing cross reference to
Section 56069), that a LAFCO proposal is "a request or statement of intention made by petition
or by resolution of application of a legislative body or of a school district proposing proceedings
for the change of organization or reorganization of a local agency as prescribed in the request or statement of
intention." Under this broad language, a "change of services" application would be considered a
LAFCO proposal.

(1)(b) Should payments for billboards, websites, mailings, radio, and newspaper
advertisements, movie theater advertisements, phone calls, and other communications to the
public that urge local citizens to oppose the "change of services" application be disclosed?

All expenditures of $100 or more must be disclosed on the campaign reports, including
expenditures for the purposes you describe.

File No. I-10-052
Page 6

(2) When do such disclosure obligations begin? Is the disclosure triggered when
expenses have been incurred or when communications are made to oppose the proposal?

As you noted in your letter, it is necessary to harmonize the recent amendments to
Section 82035.5 to existing Section 84252. Section 84252 states that filing begins "from the
time circulation of a petition begins." However, the amendment to Section 82035.5 removed the
language that limited the definition of a "proposal" to those proceedings initiated as a petition
for filing in order to expand disclosure. Thus, a literal interpretation of Section 84252 would
effectively make meaningless the amendment to Section 82035.5.

In the event of conflict between two statutes, a more recent statute will govern and take
precedence over an earlier provision. (Los Angeles Police Protective League v. City of Los
Angeles (1994) 27 Cal.App.4th 168, 178.) The rules of statutory construction require an
attempt to reconcile statutory provisions relating to the same subject matter whenever possible in
order to avoid conflict and give effect to every provision. (Taxpayers to Limit Campaign
Spending v. Fair Pol. Practices Com. (1990) 51 Cal.3d 744, 747.).

In order to harmonize these two provisions, consistent with the rules of statutory
construction, we consider the "time circulation of a petition begins" to alternatively mean "the
time that an application is filed." Since the application is considered a proceeding, the filing of
the application would be the trigger for aggregating contributions in order to qualify as a
committee. Any other construction of Section 84252 would make the more recent amendments
to Section 82035.5 meaningless.

Once these contributions equal $1,000, the coalition would be a committee. Reporting
obligations begin once the coalition becomes a committee. Please note, we have advised that
expenditures made prior to qualification as a committee may need to be recaptured on the first
campaign report, such as payments made to conduct a survey if the subsequent use of the survey
results would constitute an expenditure. (Martini Advice Letter, No. A-93-378; Winkler Advice
Letter, No. A-86-035.)

(3) Should the disclosures be filed monthly, semi annually, or quarterly?

Section 84252 provides that once the coalition qualifies as a committee, the coalition
must file all statements required for other committees under the same filing schedules. However,
in lieu of the statements required by Sections 84200 (semi-annual reports) and 84202.3 (quarterly
reports), the coalition will file monthly campaign statements from the time the application was
filed until the coalition's committee is terminated pursuant to Section 84214.

(4) What forms and types of statements should be filed?

Once the coalition qualifies as a committee, the coalition must file all statements required
for other committees under the Act. However, in lieu of the statements required by Sections
84200 (semi-annual reports) and 84202.3 (quarterly reports), the coalition will file monthly

File No. I-10-052
Page 7

campaign statements from the time the application was filed until the committee is terminated
pursuant to Section 84214.

(5) Should amounts disclosed include the payments to public relations firms and
campaign consultants, and compensation to employees when engaging in direct contacts and
grassroots lobbying when made to influence the action of LAFCO?

Yes. Once the coalition qualifies as a committee, these expenditures would be
reportable.⁵ (See e.g., Hobbs Advice Letter, No. A-09-185.)

Please note, the Commission anticipates considering clarifying regulations later this year.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5560.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

[signature]

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

JWW:jgl

___________________________

⁵ Please note, in the Hobbes Advice Letter, we also advised: "However, funds received and used for
payments to a LAFCO for fees and staff time do not constitute 'contributions' under Section 82015 that would be
applied to the threshold used to constitute a committee under the Act, because the funds are not used for a 'political
purpose.' As we said in Roberts, supra, these funds are not reportable because 'the funding of these studies is not
meant to influence the citizens to vote [or LAFCO] for or against the measure. ..., the studies simply determine
whether a proposed city is viable and considers environmental concerns. The committee is purchasing a product, the
studies, and is not influencing a vote or an election,' or a decision by a LAFCO member to support or oppose the
proposal."

QUESTION: (1)(a) Should payments for communications to the public that urge local citizens to
oppose the "change of services" application filed with the San Joaquin IApCO by the South San
Joaquin [rrigation District be disclosed under the Act even if the proposal is not initiated by a
petition?
(1Xb) Should payments for billboards, websites, mailings, radio, and newspaper
advertisements, movie theater advertisements, phone calls, and other communications to the
public that urge local citizens to oppose a "change of services" application filed with the San
Joaquin LAFCO by the South San Joaquin Irrigation Disrrict be disclosed?
(2) When do such disclosure obligations begin? Is the disclosure triggered when
expenses have been incurred or when communications are made to oppose the proposal?
(3) Should the disclosures be filed monthly, semi annually, or quarterly?
(4) What forms and types of statements should be filed?
(5) Should amounts disclosed include the payments to public relations firms and
campaign consultants, and compensation to employees when engaging in direct contacts and
grassroots lobbying when made to influence the action of LAFCo?
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 April 5, 2010 Jason Kaune Nielsen Merksamer Parrinello Mueller & Naylor, LLP 2350 Kerner Blvd, Suite 250 San Rafael, CA 94901 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-10-052 Dear Mr. Kaune: This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the San Joaquin Common Sense Coalition (a recently formed project of Citizens for Reliable and Affordable Electricity, a California public benefit nonprofit corporation, and Pacific Gas and Electric Company) regarding the campaign reporting provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please be aware that the Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions provided apply only to prospective actions. Moreover, the Commission will only provide formal written advice to a person whose duties are under the Act or to that person's authorized representative. The Commission does not provide advice to a third party about a public official's duties under the Act. (Section 83114; regulation 18329.) Thus, we cannot respond to your specific questions concerning the duties of the South San Joaquin Irrigation District. However, we can provide informal assistance with respect to your client's prospective obligations under the Act. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3).) _______________________________ ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. I-10-052 Page 2 QUESTIONS (1)(a) Should payments for communications to the public that urge local citizens to oppose the "change of services" application filed with the San Joaquin LAFCO by the South San Joaquin Irrigation District be disclosed under the Act even if the proposal is not initiated by a petition? (1)(b) Should payments for billboards, websites, mailings, radio, and newspaper advertisements, movie theater advertisements, phone calls, and other communications to the public that urge local citizens to oppose a "change of services" application filed with the San Joaquin LAFCO by the South San Joaquin Irrigation District be disclosed? (2) When do such disclosure obligations begin? Is the disclosure triggered when expenses have been incurred or when communications are made to oppose the proposal? (3) Should the disclosures be filed monthly, semi annually, or quarterly? (4) What forms and types of statements should be filed? (5) Should amounts disclosed include the payments to public relations firms and campaign consultants, and compensation to employees when engaging in direct contacts and grassroots lobbying when made to influence the action of LAFCO?
